title: Entropy from Entanglement in Quantum State Reduction
abstract: The Von Neumann entropy of reduced states is a measure of bipartite entanglement. Despite its name, the entanglement entropy cannot by itself be used as a resource for creating thermodynamic heat flows. In order to extract heat from an entangled pure state, it first needs to be converted into a stochastically mixed state by a process of quantum state reduction. Here we show that even in a system with only two degrees of freedom, for which bipartite entanglement is the sole form of entanglement available, the entanglement entropy cannot be converted into thermodynamic entropy in a one-to-one fashion. Moreover, we show that the stochastic dynamics which is necessarily present in any realistic model of quantum state reduction, allows for multiple definitions of entropy. We indicate why quantum state reduction does not allow construction of a perpetuum mobile, despite some measures of entropy evolving non-monotonically during its dynamics. Finally, we relate the different measures of entropy to the information they contain about quantum entanglement and extractable heat, and show that models of quantum state reduction based on physical, correlated stochastic driving forces give rise to observable thermodynamic signatures of quantum state reduction that can be unambiguously distinguished from environment-induced dephasing.
summary: This paper explores how entanglement is transformed into thermodynamic entropy during objective quantum state reduction. It demonstrates that this conversion is not one-to-one and proposes that the non-monotonic evolution of local entropy can serve as a signature to distinguish state reduction from environmental dephasing.
main_problem: Investigating the relationship between quantum entanglement and thermodynamic entropy during the process of objective quantum state reduction, specifically whether entanglement can be converted into thermodynamic entropy in a one-to-one fashion.
main_result: The authors show that entanglement entropy is not converted into thermodynamic entropy one-to-one, and that models with correlated noise provide a unique thermodynamic fingerprint (non-monotonic locally obtainable entropy) that distinguishes state reduction from environment-induced dephasing.
method: The study uses ensemble averaging over stochastic trajectories of a non-linear, stochastic master equation to calculate the evolution of von Neumann and thermodynamic entropies.
model_or_system: A bipartite entangled pure state (two degrees of freedom) evolving under an objective quantum state reduction model described by a stochastic, non-linear equation with a temporally correlated noise parameter.
key_observables: Thermodynamic entropy (S_td), average bipartite entanglement (S_ent), and locally obtainable entropy (S_td,int).
important_parameters: Noise correlation time (tau), strength of non-linear term (J), amplitude of stochastic term (G), and the white-noise limit (tau -> 0).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the measurement apparatus is not infinitely large and that the noise is physically correlated to allow for distinguishability from dephasing.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the evolution of a quantum state under specific stochastic parameters; Figure 2 illustrates the monotonic increase of thermodynamic entropy alongside the decrease of entanglement, the non-conservation of their sum, and the non-monotonic evolution of locally obtainable entropy in correlated noise models.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of entropy conversion, defines the stochastic reduction model, analyzes the evolution of various entropy measures, compares reduction to dephasing, and concludes with the identification of experimental signatures.
why_interesting: It provides a way to experimentally distinguish fundamental wave-function collapse models from standard decoherence by looking at thermodynamic signatures in the presence of correlated noise.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

FIG. 2. Evolution of entropy and entanglement during quantum state reduction. (a) The evolution of the instantaneous thermodynamic entropy Std(t) and the average bipartite entanglement ¯Sent(t) during quantum state reduction. (b) Sum of the thermodynamic entropy and average bipartite entanglement during quantum state reduction. The sum is not constant, indicating that entanglement is not converted into thermodynamic entropy in a one-to-one fashion. (c) The evolution of the locally obtainable entropy Std,int(t) during quantum state reduction.
